unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit, your child learns the words for shapes, colours, and clothes, and how to make things with paper shapes — like a paper quilt, a picture, or a shape animal. They talk about what people are wearing and dress up in pretend party costumes. They meet the short ‘e’ sound (hen, pen, tent) and enjoy the old story of the elves and the shoemaker. The gentle idea running through it all is that making things takes creativity and patience — and it is fun to try, try again.
unit.learningPath:
Begin with the guided teacher launch and learn a few words through pictures and play.
Listen to the Unit story and join in with repeated language.
Complete six language, speaking, early-writing and practical activities.
Meet your teacher three times each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question checkpoint and tell an adult what you can do.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A shape with four straight sides that are all the same length.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Musa draws a square on his paper.
practiceSentences: My prayer mat has a square shape.
Can you find a square in our classroom?
Look at my square, Samira!
I cut my paper into a square.
The window is shaped like a square.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: s - q - u - a - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say square slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A round shape with no corners, like the sun.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: c - i - r - c - l - e

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and point to shapes (square, circle, triangle, rectangle).

readings.title: Amal Makes a Mat
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
It was a warm afternoon in the village. Amal sat with her grandmother, Ayeeyo, under the big tree.

Ayeeyo was making a mat from soft grass. Her hands moved fast. Over, under, over, under.

“I want to make a mat too!” said Amal.

“Good,” said Ayeeyo. “Here is some grass. We will make it together.”

Amal took some grass. She started to weave. Over, under, over, under.

But her mat was not neat. It was bumpy and full of holes.

“Oh no!” said Amal. “My mat is not good.”

Her big brother Ishmael walked by. He was carrying paper and crayons.

“Do not be sad,” said Ishmael. “Look, I am making a picture with shapes. I cut a red circle and a blue square.”

He showed Amal his paper. There was a green triangle and a yellow rectangle too.

“It is beautiful,” said Amal.

“But my first picture was not so good either!” said Ishmael. He laughed. “I tried again and again.”

Amal looked at her bumpy mat. She took a deep breath.

“I will try again,” she said.

Over, under, over, under. Slow and careful this time. Amal did not rush.

Little by little, the mat grew. The holes were gone. It was flat and neat.

“Ayeeyo, look!” said Amal.

Ayeeyo smiled a big smile. “You did it, Amal! You were patient. That is how we make good things.”

Amal held up her mat. It was red and brown, like the earth.

Her little sister Hodan clapped her hands. “I want to make a mat too!”

“Come,” said Amal. “I will help you. Over, under, over, under…”

And under the big tree, the whole family sat and made things together, all afternoon long.

readings.title: Talk about Making Things
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript: Look, point and talk. This is a ___. It is a ___ ___. I am cutting ___. I am making ___. I am wearing ___. I can be a ___. An adult reads each line while the learner points, repeats and acts.

readings.title: Making Things rhyme
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
The “Party Time” poem — say it together
Party time, / Look at me. / What do you see? / I can be a clown / With a funny frown. / Or a king / With a silver ring.
Act it out! Make a funny frown for the clown. Put on a pretend crown for the king. Say it three times — a little sillier each time!
The “Higgledy Piggledy” rhyme — for the short ‘e’ sound
Higgledy Piggledy, / my black hen. / She lays eggs / for gentlemen. / Sometimes nine and sometimes ten.
Listen for the ‘e’ sound in hen, eggs, gentlemen and ten. Flap your arms like a hen while you say it!

comprehension.question: What are Amal and Ayeeyo making under the big tree?
comprehension.explanation: Ayeeyo is making a mat from soft grass and Amal says she wants one too.

comprehension.question: Which shape did Ishmael cut out in red?
comprehension.explanation: Ishmael says, 'I cut a red circle and a blue square.'

comprehension.question: Ayeeyo said Amal was patient. What did Amal do slowly?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She wove the mat slowly and carefully, without rushing
comprehension.explanation: The second time Amal weaves slow and careful, and does not rush.

comprehension.question: What did Amal do after her first mat came out bumpy?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She took a deep breath and tried again
comprehension.explanation: Amal looks at her bumpy mat, takes a deep breath and says, 'I will try again.'

comprehension.question: How did Ayeeyo feel about the finished mat? How do you know?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Pleased and proud, because she smiled a big smile
comprehension.explanation: Ayeeyo smiles a big smile and says, 'You did it, Amal! You were patient.'

comprehension.question: Why did Ishmael tell Amal about his own first picture?
comprehension.correctAnswer: To show her that trying again makes work better
comprehension.explanation: Ishmael says his first picture was not good either, and he tried again and again.

comprehension.question: Which talk line do you say while you cut paper?
comprehension.explanation: 'I am cutting ___' is the talk line for cutting.

comprehension.question: Say 'It is a ___ ___' about a red circle.
comprehension.explanation: 'It is a ___ ___' takes a colour word and a shape word.

comprehension.question: Which talk line tells what clothes you have on?
comprehension.explanation: 'I am wearing ___' is the clothes line in the talk lines.

comprehension.question: Finish this pretending line: 'I can be a ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: I can be a king. (any true idea)
comprehension.explanation: The last talk line is 'I can be a ___,' for pretending.

comprehension.question: Say 'I am making ___' about your own work today.
comprehension.correctAnswer: I am making a mat. (any true answer)
comprehension.explanation: 'I am making ___' is the talk line for the thing you are making.

comprehension.question: Hold up a shape and say, 'This is a ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: This is a square. (any shape named correctly)
comprehension.explanation: The first talk line is 'This is a ___,' for naming what you hold up.

grammar.title: Name your craft materials
grammar.explanation: Name one thing from your craft box. Say 'a' before most words, but 'an' before a vowel sound: a box, an apron.
grammar.commonMistake: Children pop 'a' before apron by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an apron, an elastic band.
grammar.memoryTip: Open-mouth sound at the start — a, e, i, o, u — means you say 'an'.
grammar.practice: Name three craft things: This is a box. This is an apron. This is a straw.

grammar.title: Put the describing word first
grammar.explanation: The describing word comes first. Say 'a' or 'an' to match its sound: a big box, an old cloth.
grammar.commonMistake: Children pop 'a' before old by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an old cloth, an empty tin.
grammar.memoryTip: Listen to the describing word. Open-mouth start — a, e, i, o, u — use 'an'.
grammar.practice: Describe three: It is a big box. It is an old cloth. It is a red straw.

grammar.title: Say what you are cutting
grammar.explanation: While you use your safe scissors, tell your teacher what you are cutting at this moment.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say 'I cutting paper.' Keep 'am' in the middle: 'I am cutting paper.'
grammar.memoryTip: Snip two fingers like scissors as you say the word 'cutting'.
grammar.practice: Say three as you work: I am cutting paper. I am cutting card. I am cutting string.

grammar.title: Tell everyone what you are making
grammar.explanation: Show your work and say what it will be. Use this while you are still busy building it.
grammar.commonMistake: Children may say 'I making a card.' The small word 'am' belongs in every sentence like this.
grammar.memoryTip: Say it while your hands are still busy, because 'making' means not finished yet.
grammar.practice: Say three about your work: I am making a card. I am making a lantern. I am making a boat.

grammar.title: Say what you are wearing
grammar.explanation: Look down at your clothes today and say one thing you have on.
grammar.commonMistake: 'I am wearing a shoes' mixes 'a' with a plural. Say 'a shoe' for one and 'shoes' for two.
grammar.memoryTip: Touch each piece of clothing as you name it.
grammar.practice: Name three clothes you have on: I am wearing a shirt. I am wearing shoes. I am wearing a cap.

grammar.title: Say the job you dream of
grammar.explanation: Name a job you dream of. Say 'a' before most jobs, but 'an' before a vowel sound: a baker, an artist.
grammar.commonMistake: Children pop 'a' before artist by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an artist, an engineer.
grammar.memoryTip: If the job word starts with an open-mouth sound — a, e, i, o, u — use 'an'.
grammar.practice: Say three jobs: I can be a baker. I can be an artist. I can be a teacher.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 - Listen and point
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: An adult holds up a shape card and names it, then you name the next one. Say: 'This is a circle.' 'This is a square.' 'This is a triangle.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern This is a ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 - Repeat the model
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Copy an adult, then describe your own shape with a colour word. Say: 'It is a red circle.' 'It is a blue square.' 'It is a big triangle.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern It is a ___ ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 - Ask and answer
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Tell your partner what you are cutting as you work. Say: 'I am cutting paper.' 'I am cutting a circle.' 'What are you cutting?'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I am cutting ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Picture talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Look at a picture of children making crafts and talk about it. Say: 'I am making a card.' 'She is making a hat.' 'We are making Eid cards.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I am making ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Role-play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Play a dressing-up game and say what you have on. Say: 'I am wearing a hat.' 'I am wearing a coat.' 'My shoes are new.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I am wearing ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - My confident turn
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Tell the class what kind of maker you would like to be. Say: 'I can be a builder.' 'I can be a baker.' 'I like making things.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I can be a ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

writing.title: Writing 1 - Draw and tell
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw something you have made from paper or blocks. Tell an adult its shape, its colour and how long it took you.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 2 - Trace
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace the words 'circle' and 'square'. Draw that shape beside each word, then say the word as you point to it.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 3 - Match and copy
writing.promptAndInstructions: Look at the pictures of scissors, glue and paper. Join each word to its picture, then copy the word you use first.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 4 - Label
writing.promptAndInstructions: Add two labels to your craft drawing: 'hat' and 'card'. Write each word right next to the thing it names.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 5 - Complete a pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Copy and finish twice: 'I am wearing ___.' Use a hat and shoes. Check that you left a space between each word.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 6 - Create one sentence
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose a picture of a job you like. Write one sentence: 'I can be a ___.' Begin with a capital letter.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

activities.title: Activity 1
activities.instructionsAndItems: Colour the shapes: the circle red, the square blue, the triangle green and the rectangle yellow. Say each shape name as you colour it.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 2
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw something you made this week. Then tell an adult: 'I made a ___. It is ___.' Show them the real thing if you still have it.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 3
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a line from each child to the shape they made: Musa to the circle, Aisha to the square, Yusuf to the triangle. Say each pair.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 4
activities.instructionsAndItems: Act out making a card: 1. fold, 2. cut, 3. glue, 4. write. Say the word for each move as you mime it.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 5
activities.instructionsAndItems: Point to four clothes: hat, coat, shoes, scarf. Say 'I can wear a ___' for each, then choose the ones for a cold day.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 6
activities.instructionsAndItems: Make an Eid card. 1. Fold the paper in half. 2. Draw a pattern of shapes. 3. Write a name inside. Share it and say what you made.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

assignments.title: My Making Things show-and-tell page
assignments.instructions: Make something with paper shapes. 1) Cut out circles, squares, triangles and rectangles. 2) Glue them into a picture, a shape animal or a paper quilt. 3) Label two parts with traced words, such as circle, gold or hat. 4) Record yourself saying: I am cutting a ___. I am making a ___. It is a blue triangle. I am wearing my ___. 5) Grown-ups, ask what was tricky and how your child kept trying, then write it down.
assignments.submissionType: Drawing or object + labels + short audio/video + reflection

quizzes.question: Which word names a shape?
quizzes.explanation: A circle is a shape; yellow is a colour, glue is a thing we use and soft tells how something feels.

quizzes.question: Which shape has three sides?
quizzes.explanation: A triangle has three sides; a circle has none and a square and rectangle have four.

quizzes.question: Which shape has four sides that are all the same length?
quizzes.explanation: All four sides of a square are equal; a triangle has three sides, a circle is round and a line is straight.

quizzes.question: Which shape is round with no corners?
quizzes.explanation: A circle is round and has no corners; the other three shapes all have corners.

quizzes.question: Which of these words tells you the colour of something?
quizzes.explanation: Green is a colour; round tells a shape, paper is a thing and cut is an action.

quizzes.question: Aisha mixes red paint with yellow paint. Which colour does she make?
quizzes.explanation: Red and yellow together make orange; blue and yellow would make green.

quizzes.question: Musa has made a red square. Which sentence describes it?
quizzes.options: It is a green triangle. | It is a blue square. | It is a red square. | It is a red circle.
quizzes.explanation: The colour and the shape must both match, so only this sentence is true.

quizzes.question: Which thing do you use to cut paper?
quizzes.explanation: Scissors cut; glue sticks, a crayon colours and a ruler helps draw straight lines.

quizzes.question: Which thing do you use to stick two pieces of paper together?
quizzes.explanation: Glue sticks things; scissors cut them apart, a pencil writes and a rubber takes writing away.

quizzes.question: Samira has a blue hat on her head. Which sentence tells us that?
quizzes.options: I am making a blue hat. | I am wearing a blue hat. | I am cutting a blue hat. | I am painting a blue hat.
quizzes.correctAnswer: I am wearing a blue hat.
quizzes.explanation: Wearing means it is on you; making, cutting and painting are things you do before it is finished.

liveSessions.title: Meet the words

liveSessions.agenda: 5 min hello song; 7 min model and repeat; 8 min game or story; 7 min guided practice; 3 min celebrate and preview
liveSessions.afterSession: Show one word, action, drawing or sentence from Making Things to an adult.

teacherNotes.note: Use 15-20 minute independent blocks inside the lesson. Read every instruction aloud, model before expecting a response, and accept pointing, acting, drawing, tracing or speaking as valid evidence.
A teacher or caregiver remains present whenever the young learner uses recording or AI tutor features. The adult controls the device and protects the child's personal information.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Answer: A mat, from soft grass. Ayeeyo is making a mat from soft grass and Amal says she wants one too. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: A circle. Ishmael says, 'I cut a red circle and a blue square.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: She wove the mat slowly and carefully, without rushing. The second time Amal weaves slow and careful, and does not rush. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: She took a deep breath and tried again. Amal looks at her bumpy mat, takes a deep breath and says, 'I will try again.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Pleased and proud, because she smiled a big smile. Ayeeyo smiles a big smile and says, 'You did it, Amal! You were patient.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.

Answer: To show her that trying again makes work better. Ishmael says his first picture was not good either, and he tried again and again. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I am cutting ___. 'I am cutting ___' is the talk line for cutting. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: It is a red circle. 'It is a ___ ___' takes a colour word and a shape word. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I am wearing ___. 'I am wearing ___' is the clothes line in the talk lines. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I can be a king. (any true idea). The last talk line is 'I can be a ___,' for pretending. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I am making a mat. (any true answer). 'I am making ___' is the talk line for the thing you are making. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: This is a square. (any shape named correctly). The first talk line is 'This is a ___,' for naming what you hold up. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “This is a / an ___.”. Model answers: This is a box. This is an apron. This is a straw. Watch for: Children pop 'a' before apron by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an apron, an elastic band.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “It is a / an ___ ___.”. Model answers: It is a big box. It is an old cloth. It is a red straw. Watch for: Children pop 'a' before old by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an old cloth, an empty tin.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I am cutting ___.”. Model answers: I am cutting paper. I am cutting card. I am cutting string. Watch for: Some children say 'I cutting paper.' Keep 'am' in the middle: 'I am cutting paper.'

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I am making ___.”. Model answers: I am making a card. I am making a lantern. I am making a boat. Watch for: Children may say 'I making a card.' The small word 'am' belongs in every sentence like this.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I am wearing ___.”. Model answers: I am wearing a shirt. I am wearing shoes. I am wearing a cap. Watch for: 'I am wearing a shoes' mixes 'a' with a plural. Say 'a shoe' for one and 'shoes' for two.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I can be a / an ___.”. Model answers: I can be a baker. I can be an artist. I can be a teacher. Watch for: Children pop 'a' before artist by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an artist, an engineer.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name and point to shapes (square, circle, triangle, rectangle).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name and point to colours (red, blue, green, yellow, silver, gold…).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name some clothes and costumes (hat, shirt, dress, clown, king…).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say “I’m cutting ______.” and “I’m making a ______.”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say “I’m wearing ______.” to describe party clothes.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can hear and say words with the short ‘e’ sound (hen, pen, ten, wet).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level1: Marks do not yet show the idea
rubrics.level3: Labels or copies a meaningful phrase
Uses a capital or end mark with support
rubrics.level4: Uses a capital and end mark independently
